--- DEBUG CLIPBOARD HTML ---
ORIGINAL:
<html>
<body>
<!--StartFragment--><html><head></head><body><h1>Transformer Architecture: A Short Report</h1><h2>Introduction</h2><p>The <strong>Transformer</strong> is a deep learning architecture introduced in the landmark paper <strong>"Attention Is All You Need"</strong> (2017). Unlike Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks, Transformers process all input tokens simultaneously using a mechanism called <strong>self-attention</strong>. This parallel processing significantly improves training speed and enables the model to capture long-range dependencies in sequences. Transformers are the foundation of modern AI systems such as GPT, BERT, T5, Llama, and Vision Transformers.</p><hr><h1>Architecture Overview</h1><p>A Transformer consists of several key components:</p><ul><li><p><strong>Input Embedding</strong></p></li><li><p><strong>Positional Encoding</strong></p></li><li><p><strong>Multi-Head Self-Attention</strong></p></li><li><p><strong>Feed Forward Neural Network</strong></p></li><li><p><strong>Residual Connections</strong></p></li><li><p><strong>Layer Normalization</strong></p></li></ul><p>A complete Transformer contains an <strong>Encoder</strong> and a <strong>Decoder</strong>, although many modern models (such as GPT) use only the decoder.</p><hr><h1>Mathematical Formulation</h1><h2>1. Input Embedding</h2><p>Each input token is converted into a dense vector representation.</p><p>[<br>X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}<br>]</p><p>where:</p><ul><li><p>(n) = number of tokens</p></li><li><p>(d) = embedding dimension</p></li></ul><hr><h2>2. Query, Key, and Value</h2><p>The input embeddings are projected into three different spaces:</p><p>[<br>Q = XW_Q<br>]</p><p>[<br>K = XW_K<br>]</p><p>[<br>V = XW_V<br>]</p><p>where:</p><ul><li><p>(Q) = Query matrix</p></li><li><p>(K) = Key matrix</p></li><li><p>(V) = Value matrix</p></li></ul><hr><h2>3. Scaled Dot-Product Attention</h2><p>The attention mechanism computes how strongly every token should attend to every other token.</p><h1>[<br>\mathrm{Attention}(Q,K,V)</h1><p>\mathrm{softmax}<br>\left(<br>\frac{QK^{T}}{\sqrt{d_k}}<br>\right)V<br>]</p><p>where:</p><ul><li><p>(QK^T) computes similarity scores.</p></li><li><p>(d_k) is the dimension of the key vectors.</p></li><li><p>Softmax converts the scores into probabilities.</p></li></ul><hr><h2>4. Multi-Head Attention</h2><p>Instead of performing attention once, Transformers perform it multiple times in parallel.</p><p>For each attention head:</p><h1>[<br>\text{head}_i</h1><p>\mathrm{Attention}(Q_i,K_i,V_i)<br>]</p><p>The outputs are concatenated:</p><h1>[<br>\mathrm{MultiHead}(Q,K,V)</h1><p>\mathrm{Concat}<br>(\text{head}_1,\ldots,\text{head}_h)W_O<br>]</p><p>where (h) is the number of attention heads.</p><hr><h2>5. Feed Forward Network</h2><p>Each token independently passes through a fully connected neural network:</p><h1>[<br>\mathrm{FFN}(x)</h1><p>\max(0,xW_1+b_1)W_2+b_2<br>]</p><p>Many modern Transformers replace the ReLU activation with <strong>GELU</strong>.</p><hr><h2>6. Positional Encoding</h2><p>Since Transformers process all tokens simultaneously, positional information is added using sinusoidal functions.</p><p>Even positions:</p><h1>[<br>PE(pos,2i)</h1><p>\sin<br>\left(<br>\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d}}<br>\right)<br>]</p><p>Odd positions:</p><h1>[<br>PE(pos,2i+1)</h1><p>\cos<br>\left(<br>\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d}}<br>\right)<br>]</p><p>where:</p><ul><li><p>(pos) = token position</p></li><li><p>(i) = embedding index</p></li></ul><hr><h2>7. Residual Connection and Layer Normalization</h2><p>Each sub-layer output is computed as</p><h1>[<br>\mathrm{Output}</h1><p>\mathrm{LayerNorm}<br>(x+\mathrm{Sublayer}(x))<br>]</p><p>Residual connections improve gradient flow, while Layer Normalization stabilizes training.</p><hr><h1>Computational Complexity</h1><p>For a sequence of length (n), self-attention requires</p><p>[<br>O(n^2)<br>]</p><p>time and memory because every token attends to every other token.</p><hr><h1>Advantages</h1><ul><li><p>Highly parallel computation</p></li><li><p>Captures long-range dependencies effectively</p></li><li><p>Eliminates sequential processing required by RNNs</p></li><li><p>Scales efficiently to billions of parameters</p></li><li><p>Achieves state-of-the-art performance across many AI tasks</p></li></ul><hr><h1>Applications</h1><p>Transformers are widely used in:</p><ul><li><p>Natural Language Processing (NLP)</p></li><li><p>Machine Translation</p></li><li><p>Text Summarization</p></li><li><p>Question Answering</p></li><li><p>Large Language Models (GPT, Llama, Claude)</p></li><li><p>Image Classification (Vision Transformers)</p></li><li><p>Speech Recognition</p></li><li><p>Multimodal AI systems</p></li></ul><hr><h1>Conclusion</h1><p>The Transformer architecture revolutionized deep learning by replacing recurrent computation with self-attention. Its ability to process entire sequences in parallel, model long-range dependencies, and scale efficiently has made it the dominant architecture in modern artificial intelligence. Core components such as <strong>scaled dot-product attention</strong>, <strong>multi-head attention</strong>, <strong>positional encoding</strong>, and <strong>feed-forward networks</strong> enable Transformers to learn rich contextual representations, making them the foundation of today's most advanced language and vision models.</p></body></html><!--EndFragment-->
</body>
</html>

TRANSFORMED:

<!--StartFragment--><h1>Transformer Architecture: A Short Report</h1><h2>Introduction</h2><p>The <strong>Transformer</strong> is a deep learning architecture introduced in the landmark paper <strong>"Attention Is All You Need"</strong> (2017). Unlike Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks, Transformers process all input tokens simultaneously using a mechanism called <strong>self-attention</strong>. This parallel processing significantly improves training speed and enables the model to capture long-range dependencies in sequences. Transformers are the foundation of modern AI systems such as GPT, BERT, T5, Llama, and Vision Transformers.</p><h1>Architecture Overview</h1><p>A Transformer consists of several key components:</p><ul><li><p><strong>Input Embedding</strong></p></li><li><p><strong>Positional Encoding</strong></p></li><li><p><strong>Multi-Head Self-Attention</strong></p></li><li><p><strong>Feed Forward Neural Network</strong></p></li><li><p><strong>Residual Connections</strong></p></li><li><p><strong>Layer Normalization</strong></p></li></ul><p>A complete Transformer contains an <strong>Encoder</strong> and a <strong>Decoder</strong>, although many modern models (such as GPT) use only the decoder.</p><h1>Mathematical Formulation</h1><h2>1. Input Embedding</h2><p>Each input token is converted into a dense vector representation.</p><p>[<br>X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}<br>]</p><p>where:</p><ul><li><p>(n) = number of tokens</p></li><li><p>(d) = embedding dimension</p></li></ul><h2>2. Query, Key, and Value</h2><p>The input embeddings are projected into three different spaces:</p><p>[<br>Q = XW_Q<br>]</p><p>[<br>K = XW_K<br>]</p><p>[<br>V = XW_V<br>]</p><p>where:</p><ul><li><p>(Q) = Query matrix</p></li><li><p>(K) = Key matrix</p></li><li><p>(V) = Value matrix</p></li></ul><h2>3. Scaled Dot-Product Attention</h2><p>The attention mechanism computes how strongly every token should attend to every other token.</p><h1>[\mathrm{Attention}(Q,K,V)</h1><p>\mathrm{softmax}<br>\left(<br>\frac{QK^{T}}{\sqrt{d_k}}<br>\right)V<br>]</p><p>where:</p><ul><li><p>(QK^T) computes similarity scores.</p></li><li><p>(d_k) is the dimension of the key vectors.</p></li><li><p>Softmax converts the scores into probabilities.</p></li></ul><h2>4. Multi-Head Attention</h2><p>Instead of performing attention once, Transformers perform it multiple times in parallel.</p><p>For each attention head:</p><h1>[\text{head}_i</h1><p>\mathrm{Attention}(Q_i,K_i,V_i)<br>]</p><p>The outputs are concatenated:</p><h1>[\mathrm{MultiHead}(Q,K,V)</h1><p>\mathrm{Concat}<br>(\text{head}_1,\ldots,\text{head}_h)W_O<br>]</p><p>where (h) is the number of attention heads.</p><h2>5. Feed Forward Network</h2><p>Each token independently passes through a fully connected neural network:</p><h1>[\mathrm{FFN}(x)</h1><p>\max(0,xW_1+b_1)W_2+b_2<br>]</p><p>Many modern Transformers replace the ReLU activation with <strong>GELU</strong>.</p><h2>6. Positional Encoding</h2><p>Since Transformers process all tokens simultaneously, positional information is added using sinusoidal functions.</p><p>Even positions:</p><h1>[PE(pos,2i)</h1><p>\sin<br>\left(<br>\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d}}<br>\right)<br>]</p><p>Odd positions:</p><h1>[PE(pos,2i+1)</h1><p>\cos<br>\left(<br>\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d}}<br>\right)<br>]</p><p>where:</p><ul><li><p>(pos) = token position</p></li><li><p>(i) = embedding index</p></li></ul><h2>7. Residual Connection and Layer Normalization</h2><p>Each sub-layer output is computed as</p><h1>[\mathrm{Output}</h1><p>\mathrm{LayerNorm}<br>(x+\mathrm{Sublayer}(x))<br>]</p><p>Residual connections improve gradient flow, while Layer Normalization stabilizes training.</p><h1>Computational Complexity</h1><p>For a sequence of length (n), self-attention requires</p><p>[<br>O(n^2)<br>]</p><p>time and memory because every token attends to every other token.</p><h1>Advantages</h1><ul><li><p>Highly parallel computation</p></li><li><p>Captures long-range dependencies effectively</p></li><li><p>Eliminates sequential processing required by RNNs</p></li><li><p>Scales efficiently to billions of parameters</p></li><li><p>Achieves state-of-the-art performance across many AI tasks</p></li></ul><h1>Applications</h1><p>Transformers are widely used in:</p><ul><li><p>Natural Language Processing (NLP)</p></li><li><p>Machine Translation</p></li><li><p>Text Summarization</p></li><li><p>Question Answering</p></li><li><p>Large Language Models (GPT, Llama, Claude)</p></li><li><p>Image Classification (Vision Transformers)</p></li><li><p>Speech Recognition</p></li><li><p>Multimodal AI systems</p></li></ul><h1>Conclusion</h1><p>The Transformer architecture revolutionized deep learning by replacing recurrent computation with self-attention. Its ability to process entire sequences in parallel, model long-range dependencies, and scale efficiently has made it the dominant architecture in modern artificial intelligence. Core components such as <strong>scaled dot-product attention</strong>, <strong>multi-head attention</strong>, <strong>positional encoding</strong>, and <strong>feed-forward networks</strong> enable Transformers to learn rich contextual representations, making them the foundation of today's most advanced language and vision models.</p>

ORIGINAL:
<html>
<body>
<!--StartFragment--><html><head></head><body><h1>Transformer Architecture</h1><h2>Introduction</h2><p>The <strong>Transformer</strong> is a deep learning architecture introduced by Ashish Vaswani and colleagues in their 2017 research paper, <em>Attention Is All You Need</em>. It marked a significant breakthrough in artificial intelligence by replacing the sequential processing used in Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks with a mechanism known as <strong>self-attention</strong>. Unlike previous models, which process one token at a time, Transformers process all input tokens simultaneously. This parallel processing greatly improves training efficiency and enables the model to capture long-range dependencies between words, making it highly effective for natural language processing (NLP) tasks. Today, the Transformer architecture serves as the foundation of many state-of-the-art AI models, including GPT, BERT, T5, Llama, and Vision Transformers.</p><h2>Architecture of the Transformer</h2><p>A standard Transformer consists of two major components: the <strong>Encoder</strong> and the <strong>Decoder</strong>. The encoder is responsible for reading and understanding the input sequence, while the decoder generates the output sequence based on the encoder's representations. Each encoder and decoder layer contains multiple sub-components, including <strong>Multi-Head Self-Attention</strong>, <strong>Feed Forward Neural Networks</strong>, <strong>Residual Connections</strong>, and <strong>Layer Normalization</strong>. The input tokens are first converted into numerical vectors using an embedding layer, after which positional information is added to preserve the order of words. These enriched embeddings are then processed through multiple stacked layers, allowing the model to learn increasingly complex relationships between tokens.</p><h2>Self-Attention Mechanism</h2><p>The most important innovation in the Transformer architecture is the <strong>Self-Attention Mechanism</strong>. Self-attention enables every word in a sentence to consider every other word while computing its representation. Instead of focusing only on nearby words, the model learns which words are most relevant to one another regardless of their positions in the sequence.</p><p>To compute self-attention, the input matrix (X) is projected into three different matrices known as the <strong>Query (Q)</strong>, <strong>Key (K)</strong>, and <strong>Value (V)</strong> matrices:</p><p>[<br>Q = XW_Q<br>]</p><p>[<br>K = XW_K<br>]</p><p>[<br>V = XW_V<br>]</p><p>where:</p><ul><li><p>(X) is the input embedding matrix.</p></li><li><p>(W_Q), (W_K), and (W_V) are learnable weight matrices.</p></li><li><p>(Q), (K), and (V) represent the queries, keys, and values, respectively.</p></li></ul><p>The similarity between tokens is computed using the <strong>Scaled Dot-Product Attention</strong> equation:</p><h1>[<br>\text{Attention}(Q,K,V)</h1><p>\text{softmax}<br>\left(<br>\frac{QK^{T}}{\sqrt{d_k}}<br>\right)V<br>]</p><p>where (d_k) is the dimension of the key vectors. The dot product (QK^T) measures the similarity between queries and keys, while the softmax function converts these similarities into probabilities that determine how much attention each token should receive. The resulting weighted values produce the contextual representation of each token.</p><h2>Multi-Head Attention</h2><p>Instead of performing self-attention only once, the Transformer performs it multiple times in parallel using <strong>Multi-Head Attention</strong>. Each attention head learns different relationships within the same sentence. For example, one head may focus on grammatical structure while another captures semantic meaning.</p><p>For each attention head:</p><h1>[<br>\text{Head}_i</h1><p>\text{Attention}(Q_i,K_i,V_i)<br>]</p><p>The outputs of all attention heads are concatenated and projected to form the final output:</p><h1>[<br>\text{MultiHead}(Q,K,V)</h1><p>\text{Concat}<br>(\text{Head}_1,\text{Head}_2,\ldots,\text{Head}_h)W_O<br>]</p><p>where (h) denotes the number of attention heads and (W_O) is a learnable output weight matrix.</p><h2>Feed Forward Neural Network</h2><p>After the attention mechanism, each token passes independently through a <strong>Feed Forward Neural Network (FFN)</strong>. This network introduces additional non-linearity and enables the model to learn more complex patterns.</p><p>The FFN is mathematically represented as:</p><h1>[<br>\text{FFN}(x)</h1><p>\max(0,xW_1+b_1)W_2+b_2<br>]</p><p>where (W_1) and (W_2) are weight matrices, while (b_1) and (b_2) are bias vectors. Many modern Transformer models replace the ReLU activation function with the <strong>Gaussian Error Linear Unit (GELU)</strong> for improved performance.</p><h2>Positional Encoding</h2><p>Since Transformers process all words simultaneously rather than sequentially, they require additional information to understand the order of words. This is achieved through <strong>Positional Encoding</strong>, which adds unique positional values to each word embedding.</p><p>The positional encoding is computed using sinusoidal functions:</p><p>For even dimensions:</p><h1>[<br>PE(pos,2i)</h1><p>\sin<br>\left(<br>\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d}}<br>\right)<br>]</p><p>For odd dimensions:</p><h1>[<br>PE(pos,2i+1)</h1><p>\cos<br>\left(<br>\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d}}<br>\right)<br>]</p><p>where (pos) represents the token position, (i) denotes the embedding dimension, and (d) is the embedding size. These sinusoidal patterns allow the model to identify the relative positions of words within a sequence.</p><h2>Residual Connections and Layer Normalization</h2><p>To improve training stability and facilitate gradient propagation, each sub-layer in the Transformer uses <strong>Residual Connections</strong> followed by <strong>Layer Normalization</strong>. The output of each sub-layer is computed as:</p><h1>[<br>\text{Output}</h1><p>\text{LayerNorm}<br>(x+\text{Sublayer}(x))<br>]</p><p>Residual connections help prevent the vanishing gradient problem, while layer normalization stabilizes the learning process and accelerates convergence during training.</p><h2>Advantages of the Transformer</h2><p>The Transformer architecture offers several significant advantages over earlier sequence models. Since it processes all tokens in parallel, it achieves much faster training than RNNs and LSTMs. The self-attention mechanism enables the model to capture long-range dependencies effectively, allowing it to understand relationships between words that are far apart in a sentence. Transformers also scale efficiently to extremely large datasets and model sizes, making them ideal for training modern large language models with billions of parameters. Furthermore, the architecture is highly versatile and has been successfully adapted for applications in natural language processing, computer vision, speech recognition, reinforcement learning, and multimodal artificial intelligence.</p><h2>Applications</h2><p>Transformers are now the dominant architecture across numerous AI applications. They are widely used for machine translation, text summarization, sentiment analysis, question answering, document classification, conversational AI, code generation, speech recognition, image classification through Vision Transformers (ViTs), and multimodal systems that process text, images, audio, and video together. Popular models such as GPT, BERT, T5, Llama, and CLIP all rely on Transformer-based architectures.</p><h2>Conclusion</h2><p>The Transformer architecture has fundamentally transformed the field of deep learning by introducing the self-attention mechanism and eliminating the need for recurrent computations. Its ability to process sequences in parallel, capture complex contextual relationships, and scale efficiently has made it the backbone of modern artificial intelligence. Mathematical concepts such as <strong>Query-Key-Value projections</strong>, <strong>Scaled Dot-Product Attention</strong>, <strong>Multi-Head Attention</strong>, <strong>Feed Forward Networks</strong>, and <strong>Positional Encoding</strong> work together to enable Transformers to learn rich and meaningful representations of data. As a result, Transformers continue to drive advancements in natural language processing, computer vision, speech technologies, and many other domains, making them one of the most influential innovations in modern machine learning.</p></body></html><!--EndFragment-->
</body>
</html>

<!--StartFragment--><h1>Transformer Architecture</h1><h2>Introduction</h2><p>The <strong>Transformer</strong> is a deep learning architecture introduced by Ashish Vaswani and colleagues in their 2017 research paper, <em>Attention Is All You Need</em>. It marked a significant breakthrough in artificial intelligence by replacing the sequential processing used in Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks with a mechanism known as <strong>self-attention</strong>. Unlike previous models, which process one token at a time, Transformers process all input tokens simultaneously. This parallel processing greatly improves training efficiency and enables the model to capture long-range dependencies between words, making it highly effective for natural language processing (NLP) tasks. Today, the Transformer architecture serves as the foundation of many state-of-the-art AI models, including GPT, BERT, T5, Llama, and Vision Transformers.</p><h2>Architecture of the Transformer</h2><p>A standard Transformer consists of two major components: the <strong>Encoder</strong> and the <strong>Decoder</strong>. The encoder is responsible for reading and understanding the input sequence, while the decoder generates the output sequence based on the encoder's representations. Each encoder and decoder layer contains multiple sub-components, including <strong>Multi-Head Self-Attention</strong>, <strong>Feed Forward Neural Networks</strong>, <strong>Residual Connections</strong>, and <strong>Layer Normalization</strong>. The input tokens are first converted into numerical vectors using an embedding layer, after which positional information is added to preserve the order of words. These enriched embeddings are then processed through multiple stacked layers, allowing the model to learn increasingly complex relationships between tokens.</p><h2>Self-Attention Mechanism</h2><p>The most important innovation in the Transformer architecture is the <strong>Self-Attention Mechanism</strong>. Self-attention enables every word in a sentence to consider every other word while computing its representation. Instead of focusing only on nearby words, the model learns which words are most relevant to one another regardless of their positions in the sequence.</p><p>To compute self-attention, the input matrix (X) is projected into three different matrices known as the <strong>Query (Q)</strong>, <strong>Key (K)</strong>, and <strong>Value (V)</strong> matrices:</p><p>[<br>Q = XW_Q<br>]</p><p>[<br>K = XW_K<br>]</p><p>[<br>V = XW_V<br>]</p><p>where:</p><ul><li><p>(X) is the input embedding matrix.</p></li><li><p>(W_Q), (W_K), and (W_V) are learnable weight matrices.</p></li><li><p>(Q), (K), and (V) represent the queries, keys, and values, respectively.</p></li></ul><p>The similarity between tokens is computed using the <strong>Scaled Dot-Product Attention</strong> equation:</p><h1>[\text{Attention}(Q,K,V)</h1><p>\text{softmax}<br>\left(<br>\frac{QK^{T}}{\sqrt{d_k}}<br>\right)V<br>]</p><p>where (d_k) is the dimension of the key vectors. The dot product (QK^T) measures the similarity between queries and keys, while the softmax function converts these similarities into probabilities that determine how much attention each token should receive. The resulting weighted values produce the contextual representation of each token.</p><h2>Multi-Head Attention</h2><p>Instead of performing self-attention only once, the Transformer performs it multiple times in parallel using <strong>Multi-Head Attention</strong>. Each attention head learns different relationships within the same sentence. For example, one head may focus on grammatical structure while another captures semantic meaning.</p><p>For each attention head:</p><h1>[\text{Head}_i</h1><p>\text{Attention}(Q_i,K_i,V_i)<br>]</p><p>The outputs of all attention heads are concatenated and projected to form the final output:</p><h1>[\text{MultiHead}(Q,K,V)</h1><p>\text{Concat}<br>(\text{Head}_1,\text{Head}_2,\ldots,\text{Head}_h)W_O<br>]</p><p>where (h) denotes the number of attention heads and (W_O) is a learnable output weight matrix.</p><h2>Feed Forward Neural Network</h2><p>After the attention mechanism, each token passes independently through a <strong>Feed Forward Neural Network (FFN)</strong>. This network introduces additional non-linearity and enables the model to learn more complex patterns.</p><p>The FFN is mathematically represented as:</p><h1>[\text{FFN}(x)</h1><p>\max(0,xW_1+b_1)W_2+b_2<br>]</p><p>where (W_1) and (W_2) are weight matrices, while (b_1) and (b_2) are bias vectors. Many modern Transformer models replace the ReLU activation function with the <strong>Gaussian Error Linear Unit (GELU)</strong> for improved performance.</p><h2>Positional Encoding</h2><p>Since Transformers process all words simultaneously rather than sequentially, they require additional information to understand the order of words. This is achieved through <strong>Positional Encoding</strong>, which adds unique positional values to each word embedding.</p><p>The positional encoding is computed using sinusoidal functions:</p><p>For even dimensions:</p><h1>[PE(pos,2i)</h1><p>\sin<br>\left(<br>\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d}}<br>\right)<br>]</p><p>For odd dimensions:</p><h1>[PE(pos,2i+1)</h1><p>\cos<br>\left(<br>\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d}}<br>\right)<br>]</p><p>where (pos) represents the token position, (i) denotes the embedding dimension, and (d) is the embedding size. These sinusoidal patterns allow the model to identify the relative positions of words within a sequence.</p><h2>Residual Connections and Layer Normalization</h2><p>To improve training stability and facilitate gradient propagation, each sub-layer in the Transformer uses <strong>Residual Connections</strong> followed by <strong>Layer Normalization</strong>. The output of each sub-layer is computed as:</p><h1>[\text{Output}</h1><p>\text{LayerNorm}<br>(x+\text{Sublayer}(x))<br>]</p><p>Residual connections help prevent the vanishing gradient problem, while layer normalization stabilizes the learning process and accelerates convergence during training.</p><h2>Advantages of the Transformer</h2><p>The Transformer architecture offers several significant advantages over earlier sequence models. Since it processes all tokens in parallel, it achieves much faster training than RNNs and LSTMs. The self-attention mechanism enables the model to capture long-range dependencies effectively, allowing it to understand relationships between words that are far apart in a sentence. Transformers also scale efficiently to extremely large datasets and model sizes, making them ideal for training modern large language models with billions of parameters. Furthermore, the architecture is highly versatile and has been successfully adapted for applications in natural language processing, computer vision, speech recognition, reinforcement learning, and multimodal artificial intelligence.</p><h2>Applications</h2><p>Transformers are now the dominant architecture across numerous AI applications. They are widely used for machine translation, text summarization, sentiment analysis, question answering, document classification, conversational AI, code generation, speech recognition, image classification through Vision Transformers (ViTs), and multimodal systems that process text, images, audio, and video together. Popular models such as GPT, BERT, T5, Llama, and CLIP all rely on Transformer-based architectures.</p><h2>Conclusion</h2><p>The Transformer architecture has fundamentally transformed the field of deep learning by introducing the self-attention mechanism and eliminating the need for recurrent computations. Its ability to process sequences in parallel, capture complex contextual relationships, and scale efficiently has made it the backbone of modern artificial intelligence. Mathematical concepts such as <strong>Query-Key-Value projections</strong>, <strong>Scaled Dot-Product Attention</strong>, <strong>Multi-Head Attention</strong>, <strong>Feed Forward Networks</strong>, and <strong>Positional Encoding</strong> work together to enable Transformers to learn rich and meaningful representations of data. As a result, Transformers continue to drive advancements in natural language processing, computer vision, speech technologies, and many other domains, making them one of the most influential innovations in modern machine learning.</p>

<!--StartFragment--><html><head></head><body><p>If you're testing an academic formatter, parser, or paste engine, you want reports that stress different formatting patterns. A report on Transformers is only one case. The real challenge comes from documents containing equations, lists, tables, code, references, figures, and mixed formatting. Because apparently no two academic authors can agree on how to write a paper.</p><p>Here are some excellent topics:</p><h3>1. Linear Regression</h3><p><strong>Contains:</strong></p><ul><li><p>Matrix equations</p></li><li><p>Statistics</p></li><li><p>Greek symbols</p></li><li><p>Tables</p></li><li><p>Numbered equations</p></li></ul><p><strong>Equations:</strong><br>[<br>y = \beta_0 + \beta_1x + \epsilon<br>]</p><p>[<br>\hat{\beta}=(X^TX)^{-1}X^Ty<br>]</p><hr><h3>2. Logistic Regression</h3><p><strong>Contains:</strong></p><ul><li><p>Probability equations</p></li><li><p>Sigmoid function</p></li><li><p>Loss function</p></li><li><p>Binary classification</p></li></ul><p><strong>Equations</strong></p><p>[<br>P(y=1|x)=\frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}<br>]</p><p>[<br>J(\theta)=<br>-\frac1m<br>\sum_{i=1}^{m}<br>\left[<br>y_i\log(h_\theta(x_i))<br>+<br>(1-y_i)\log(1-h_\theta(x_i))<br>\right]<br>]</p><hr><h3>3. Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN)</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Images</p></li><li><p>Kernels</p></li><li><p>Feature maps</p></li><li><p>Convolution equations</p></li></ul><p>Equation</p><h1>[<br>S(i,j)</h1><h1>(X*K)(i,j)</h1><p>\sum_m\sum_n<br>X(i+m,j+n)K(m,n)<br>]</p><hr><h3>4. Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN)</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Time-series equations</p></li><li><p>Recursive formulas</p></li><li><p>Hidden states</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>h_t</h1><p>\tanh(W_hh_{t-1}+W_xx_t+b)<br>]</p><hr><h3>5. Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Four gate equations</p></li><li><p>Multiple matrices</p></li><li><p>Sigmoid</p></li><li><p>tanh</p></li></ul><p>Example</p><p>[<br>f_t=\sigma(W_fx_t+U_fh_{t-1}+b_f)<br>]</p><p>[<br>c_t=f_t\odot c_{t-1}+i_t\odot\tilde c_t<br>]</p><hr><h3>6. Gradient Descent</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Optimization equations</p></li><li><p>Algorithm steps</p></li><li><p>Mathematical notation</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>\theta</h1><h2>\theta</h2><p>\alpha<br>\nabla J(\theta)<br>]</p><hr><h3>7. Support Vector Machines (SVM)</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Optimization</p></li><li><p>Constraints</p></li><li><p>Kernel functions</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>f(x)</h1><p>w^Tx+b<br>]</p><hr><h3>8. Principal Component Analysis (PCA)</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Eigenvalues</p></li><li><p>Covariance matrices</p></li><li><p>Matrix algebra</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>C</h1><p>\frac1nX^TX<br>]</p><p>[<br>Cv=\lambda v<br>]</p><hr><h3>9. K-Means Clustering</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Algorithm</p></li><li><p>Distance equations</p></li><li><p>Iterative procedure</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>J</h1><p>\sum_{i=1}^{k}<br>\sum_{x\in C_i}<br>|x-\mu_i|^2<br>]</p><hr><h3>10. Decision Trees</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Information Gain</p></li><li><p>Entropy</p></li><li><p>Algorithms</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>Entropy(S)</h1><ul><li></li></ul><p>\sum_ip_i\log_2p_i<br>]</p><hr><h3>11. Naive Bayes</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Bayes theorem</p></li><li><p>Conditional probability</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>P(A|B)</h1><p>\frac{P(B|A)P(A)}{P(B)}<br>]</p><hr><h3>12. Graph Neural Networks (GNN)</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Graph notation</p></li><li><p>Matrix equations</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>H^{(l+1)}</h1><p>\sigma<br>(<br>\hat D^{-1/2}<br>\hat A<br>\hat D^{-1/2}<br>H^{(l)}<br>W^{(l)}<br>)<br>]</p><hr><h3>13. Diffusion Models</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Probability</p></li><li><p>Noise schedules</p></li><li><p>Gaussian equations</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>q(x_t|x_{t-1})</h1><p>\mathcal N<br>(<br>\sqrt{1-\beta_t}x_{t-1},<br>\beta_tI<br>)<br>]</p><hr><h3>14. Generative Adversarial Networks (GAN)</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Min-max optimization</p></li><li><p>Probability</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>\min_G<br>\max_D<br>V(D,G)</h1><p>\mathbb E[\log D(x)]<br>+<br>\mathbb E[\log(1-D(G(z)))]<br>]</p><hr><h3>15. Reinforcement Learning</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Bellman equations</p></li><li><p>Policies</p></li><li><p>Rewards</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>Q(s,a)</h1><p>r<br>+<br>\gamma<br>\max_{a'}<br>Q(s',a')<br>]</p><hr><h3>16. Quantum Computing</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Dirac notation</p></li><li><p>Matrices</p></li><li><p>Complex numbers</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>|\psi\rangle</h1><p>\alpha|0\rangle<br>+<br>\beta|1\rangle<br>]</p><hr><h3>17. Fourier Transform</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Integrals</p></li><li><p>Complex exponentials</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>F(\omega)</h1><p>\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}<br>f(t)e^{-j\omega t}dt<br>]</p><hr><h3>18. AC Induction Motor</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Engineering equations</p></li><li><p>Tables</p></li><li><p>Diagrams</p></li><li><p>Symbols</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>N_s</h1><p>\frac{120f}{P}<br>]</p><h1>[<br>s</h1><p>\frac{N_s-N_r}{N_s}<br>]</p><hr><h3>19. Software Testing</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>UML diagrams</p></li><li><p>Tables</p></li><li><p>Bullet lists</p></li><li><p>Process flows</p></li><li><p>IEEE references</p></li></ul><p>Little mathematical content, making it a good contrast to AI-heavy papers.</p><hr><h3>20. Operating Systems</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Scheduling algorithms</p></li><li><p>Code blocks</p></li><li><p>Tables</p></li><li><p>Memory management formulas</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>Turnaround</h1><h2>Completion</h2><p>Arrival<br>]</p><hr><h2>For stress-testing your formatter</h2><p>If your goal is to build something like FormatMagic or a robust academic paste engine, I'd assemble a corpus rather than rely on a handful of reports. Include documents that exercise different structures:</p><table><thead><tr><th>Document Type</th><th>Tests</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td>Transformer Architecture</td><td>Inline math, display math, headings</td></tr><tr><td>Linear Regression</td><td>Matrix equations</td></tr><tr><td>CNN</td><td>Figures, equations</td></tr><tr><td>Software Testing</td><td>Tables, nested lists</td></tr><tr><td>AC Induction Motor</td><td>Engineering formulas, units</td></tr><tr><td>Research Paper (IEEE)</td><td>References, citations, two-column layout</td></tr><tr><td>Thesis Chapter</td><td>Long sections, footnotes</td></tr><tr><td>Markdown README</td><td>Fenced code, lists</td></tr><tr><td>Programming Tutorial</td><td>Syntax-highlighted code blocks</td></tr><tr><td>Physics Notes</td><td>Integrals, summations, Greek symbols</td></tr><tr><td>Chemistry Notes</td><td>Chemical formulas, subscripts</td></tr><tr><td>Economics Paper</td><td>Tables, percentages, charts</td></tr><tr><td>Statistics Notes</td><td>Probability notation and distributions</td></tr></tbody></table><p>A formatter that can faithfully handle all of those is much closer to a production-quality document pipeline than one that only succeeds on neatly formatted AI reports.</p></body></html><!--EndFragment-->
</body>
</html>

TRANSFORMED:

<!--StartFragment--><p>If you're testing an academic formatter, parser, or paste engine, you want reports that stress different formatting patterns. A report on Transformers is only one case. The real challenge comes from documents containing equations, lists, tables, code, references, figures, and mixed formatting. Because apparently no two academic authors can agree on how to write a paper.</p><p>Here are some excellent topics:</p><h3>1. Linear Regression</h3><p><strong>Contains:</strong></p><ul><li><p>Matrix equations</p></li><li><p>Statistics</p></li><li><p>Greek symbols</p></li><li><p>Tables</p></li><li><p>Numbered equations</p></li></ul><p><strong>Equations:</strong><br>[<br>y = \beta_0 + \beta_1x + \epsilon<br>]</p><p>[<br>\hat{\beta}=(X^TX)^{-1}X^Ty<br>]</p><h3>2. Logistic Regression</h3><p><strong>Contains:</strong></p><ul><li><p>Probability equations</p></li><li><p>Sigmoid function</p></li><li><p>Loss function</p></li><li><p>Binary classification</p></li></ul><p><strong>Equations</strong></p><p>[<br>P(y=1|x)=\frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}<br>]</p><p>[<br>J(\theta)=<br>-\frac1m<br>\sum_{i=1}^{m}<br>\left[<br>y_i\log(h_\theta(x_i))<br>+<br>(1-y_i)\log(1-h_\theta(x_i))<br>\right]<br>]</p><h3>3. Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN)</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Images</p></li><li><p>Kernels</p></li><li><p>Feature maps</p></li><li><p>Convolution equations</p></li></ul><p>Equation</p><h1>[S(i,j)</h1><h1>(X*K)(i,j)</h1><p>\sum_m\sum_n<br>X(i+m,j+n)K(m,n)<br>]</p><h3>4. Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN)</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Time-series equations</p></li><li><p>Recursive formulas</p></li><li><p>Hidden states</p></li></ul><h1>[h_t</h1><p>\tanh(W_hh_{t-1}+W_xx_t+b)<br>]</p><h3>5. Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Four gate equations</p></li><li><p>Multiple matrices</p></li><li><p>Sigmoid</p></li><li><p>tanh</p></li></ul><p>Example</p><p>[<br>f_t=\sigma(W_fx_t+U_fh_{t-1}+b_f)<br>]</p><p>[<br>c_t=f_t\odot c_{t-1}+i_t\odot\tilde c_t<br>]</p><h3>6. Gradient Descent</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Optimization equations</p></li><li><p>Algorithm steps</p></li><li><p>Mathematical notation</p></li></ul><h1>[\theta</h1><h2>\theta</h2><p>\alpha<br>\nabla J(\theta)<br>]</p><h3>7. Support Vector Machines (SVM)</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Optimization</p></li><li><p>Constraints</p></li><li><p>Kernel functions</p></li></ul><h1>[f(x)</h1><p>w^Tx+b<br>]</p><h3>8. Principal Component Analysis (PCA)</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Eigenvalues</p></li><li><p>Covariance matrices</p></li><li><p>Matrix algebra</p></li></ul><h1>[C</h1><p>\frac1nX^TX<br>]</p><p>[<br>Cv=\lambda v<br>]</p><h3>9. K-Means Clustering</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Algorithm</p></li><li><p>Distance equations</p></li><li><p>Iterative procedure</p></li></ul><h1>[J</h1><p>\sum_{i=1}^{k}<br>\sum_{x\in C_i}<br>|x-\mu_i|^2<br>]</p><h3>10. Decision Trees</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Information Gain</p></li><li><p>Entropy</p></li><li><p>Algorithms</p></li></ul><h1>[Entropy(S)</h1><ul><li></li></ul><p>\sum_ip_i\log_2p_i<br>]</p><h3>11. Naive Bayes</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Bayes theorem</p></li><li><p>Conditional probability</p></li></ul><h1>[P(A|B)</h1><p>\frac{P(B|A)P(A)}{P(B)}<br>]</p><h3>12. Graph Neural Networks (GNN)</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Graph notation</p></li><li><p>Matrix equations</p></li></ul><h1>[H^{(l+1)}</h1><p>\sigma<br>(<br>\hat D^{-1/2}<br>\hat A<br>\hat D^{-1/2}<br>H^{(l)}<br>W^{(l)}<br>)<br>]</p><h3>13. Diffusion Models</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Probability</p></li><li><p>Noise schedules</p></li><li><p>Gaussian equations</p></li></ul><h1>[q(x_t|x_{t-1})</h1><p>\mathcal N<br>(<br>\sqrt{1-\beta_t}x_{t-1},<br>\beta_tI<br>)<br>]</p><h3>14. Generative Adversarial Networks (GAN)</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Min-max optimization</p></li><li><p>Probability</p></li></ul><h1>[\min_G\max_DV(D,G)</h1><p>\mathbb E[\log D(x)]<br>+<br>\mathbb E[\log(1-D(G(z)))]<br>]</p><h3>15. Reinforcement Learning</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Bellman equations</p></li><li><p>Policies</p></li><li><p>Rewards</p></li></ul><h1>[Q(s,a)</h1><p>r<br>+<br>\gamma<br>\max_{a'}<br>Q(s',a')<br>]</p><h3>16. Quantum Computing</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Dirac notation</p></li><li><p>Matrices</p></li><li><p>Complex numbers</p></li></ul><h1>[|\psi\rangle</h1><p>\alpha|0\rangle<br>+<br>\beta|1\rangle<br>]</p><h3>17. Fourier Transform</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Integrals</p></li><li><p>Complex exponentials</p></li></ul><h1>[F(\omega)</h1><p>\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}<br>f(t)e^{-j\omega t}dt<br>]</p><h3>18. AC Induction Motor</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Engineering equations</p></li><li><p>Tables</p></li><li><p>Diagrams</p></li><li><p>Symbols</p></li></ul><h1>[N_s</h1><p>\frac{120f}{P}<br>]</p><h1>[s</h1><p>\frac{N_s-N_r}{N_s}<br>]</p><h3>19. Software Testing</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>UML diagrams</p></li><li><p>Tables</p></li><li><p>Bullet lists</p></li><li><p>Process flows</p></li><li><p>IEEE references</p></li></ul><p>Little mathematical content, making it a good contrast to AI-heavy papers.</p><h3>20. Operating Systems</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Scheduling algorithms</p></li><li><p>Code blocks</p></li><li><p>Tables</p></li><li><p>Memory management formulas</p></li></ul><h1>[Turnaround</h1><h2>Completion</h2><p>Arrival<br>]</p><h2>For stress-testing your formatter</h2><p>If your goal is to build something like FormatMagic or a robust academic paste engine, I'd assemble a corpus rather than rely on a handful of reports. Include documents that exercise different structures:</p><table><thead><tr><th>Document Type</th><th>Tests</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td>Transformer Architecture</td><td>Inline math, display math, headings</td></tr><tr><td>Linear Regression</td><td>Matrix equations</td></tr><tr><td>CNN</td><td>Figures, equations</td></tr><tr><td>Software Testing</td><td>Tables, nested lists</td></tr><tr><td>AC Induction Motor</td><td>Engineering formulas, units</td></tr><tr><td>Research Paper (IEEE)</td><td>References, citations, two-column layout</td></tr><tr><td>Thesis Chapter</td><td>Long sections, footnotes</td></tr><tr><td>Markdown README</td><td>Fenced code, lists</td></tr><tr><td>Programming Tutorial</td><td>Syntax-highlighted code blocks</td></tr><tr><td>Physics Notes</td><td>Integrals, summations, Greek symbols</td></tr><tr><td>Chemistry Notes</td><td>Chemical formulas, subscripts</td></tr><tr><td>Economics Paper</td><td>Tables, percentages, charts</td></tr><tr><td>Statistics Notes</td><td>Probability notation and distributions</td></tr></tbody></table><p>A formatter that can faithfully handle all of those is much closer to a production-quality document pipeline than one that only succeeds on neatly formatted AI reports.</p>

Support Vector Machines (SVM)</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Optimization</p></li><li><p>Constraints</p></li><li><p>Kernel functions</p></li></ul><h1>[f(x)</h1><p>w^Tx+b<br>]</p><h3>8. Principal Component Analysis (PCA)</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Eigenvalues</p></li><li><p>Covariance matrices</p></li><li><p>Matrix algebra</p></li></ul><h1>[C</h1><p>\frac1nX^TX<br>]</p><p>[<br>Cv=\lambda v<br>]</p><h3>9. K-Means Clustering</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Algorithm</p></li><li><p>Distance equations</p></li><li><p>Iterative procedure</p></li></ul><h1>[J</h1><p>\sum_{i=1}^{k}<br>\sum_{x\in C_i}<br>|x-\mu_i|^2<br>]</p><h3>10. Decision Trees</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Information Gain</p></li><li><p>Entropy</p></li><li><p>Algorithms</p></li></ul><h1>[Entropy(S)</h1><ul><li></li></ul><p>\sum_ip_i\log_2p_i<br>]</p><h3>11. Naive Bayes</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Bayes theorem</p></li><li><p>Conditional probability</p></li></ul><h1>[P(A|B)</h1><p>\frac{P(B|A)P(A)}{P(B)}<br>]</p><h3>12. Graph Neural Networks (GNN)</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Graph notation</p></li><li><p>Matrix equations</p></li></ul><h1>[H^{(l+1)}</h1><p>\sigma<br>(<br>\hat D^{-1/2}<br>\hat A<br>\hat D^{-1/2}<br>H^{(l)}<br>W^{(l)}<br>)<br>]</p><h3>13. Diffusion Models</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Probability</p></li><li><p>Noise schedules</p></li><li><p>Gaussian equations</p></li></ul><h1>[q(x_t|x_{t-1})</h1><p>\mathcal N<br>(<br>\sqrt{1-\beta_t}x_{t-1},<br>\beta_tI<br>)<br>]</p><h3>14. Generative Adversarial Networks (GAN)</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Min-max optimization</p></li><li><p>Probability</p></li></ul><h1>[\min_G\max_DV(D,G)</h1><p>\mathbb E[\log D(x)]<br>+<br>\mathbb E[\log(1-D(G(z)))]<br>]</p><h3>15. Reinforcement Learning</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Bellman equations</p></li><li><p>Policies</p></li><li><p>Rewards</p></li></ul><h1>[Q(s,a)</h1><p>r<br>+<br>\gamma<br>\max_{a'}<br>Q(s',a')<br>]</p><h3>16. Quantum Computing</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Dirac notation</p></li><li><p>Matrices</p></li><li><p>Complex numbers</p></li></ul><h1>[|\psi\rangle</h1><p>\alpha|0\rangle<br>+<br>\beta|1\rangle<br>]</p><h3>17. Fourier Transform</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Integrals</p></li><li><p>Complex exponentials</p></li></ul><h1>[F(\omega)</h1><p>\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}<br>f(t)e^{-j\omega t}dt<br>]</p><h3>18. AC Induction Motor</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Engineering equations</p></li><li><p>Tables</p></li><li><p>Diagrams</p></li><li><p>Symbols</p></li></ul><h1>[N_s</h1><p>\frac{120f}{P}<br>]</p><h1>[s</h1><p>\frac{N_s-N_r}{N_s}<br>]</p><h3>19. Software Testing</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>UML diagrams</p></li><li><p>Tables</p></li><li><p>Bullet lists</p></li><li><p>Process flows</p></li><li><p>IEEE references</p></li></ul><p>Little mathematical content, making it a good contrast to AI-heavy papers.</p><h3>20. Operating Systems</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Scheduling algorithms</p></li><li><p>Code blocks</p></li><li><p>Tables</p></li><li><p>Memory management formulas</p></li></ul><h1>[Turnaround</h1><h2>Completion</h2><p>Arrival<br>]</p><h2>For stress-testing your formatter</h2><p>If your goal is to build something like FormatMagic or a robust academic paste engine, I'd assemble a corpus rather than rely on a handful of reports. Include documents that exercise different structures:</p><table><thead><tr><th>Document Type</th><th>Tests</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td>Transformer Architecture</td><td>Inline math, display math, headings</td></tr><tr><td>Linear Regression</td><td>Matrix equations</td></tr><tr><td>CNN</td><td>Figures, equations</td></tr><tr><td>Software Testing</td><td>Tables, nested lists</td></tr><tr><td>AC Induction Motor</td><td>Engineering formulas, units</td></tr><tr><td>Research Paper (IEEE)</td><td>References, citations, two-column layout</td></tr><tr><td>Thesis Chapter</td><td>Long sections, footnotes</td></tr><tr><td>Markdown README</td><td>Fenced code, lists</td></tr><tr><td>Programming Tutorial</td><td>Syntax-highlighted code blocks</td></tr><tr><td>Physics Notes</td><td>Integrals, summations, Greek symbols</td></tr><tr><td>Chemistry Notes</td><td>Chemical formulas, subscripts</td></tr><tr><td>Economics Paper</td><td>Tables, percentages, charts</td></tr><tr><td>Statistics Notes</td><td>Probability notation and distributions</td></tr></tbody></table><p>A formatter that can faithfully handle all of those is much closer to a production-quality document pipeline than one that only succeeds on neatly formatted AI reports.</p>

<!--StartFragment--><html><head></head><body><p>If you're testing an academic formatter, parser, or paste engine, you want reports that stress different formatting patterns. A report on Transformers is only one case. The real challenge comes from documents containing equations, lists, tables, code, references, figures, and mixed formatting. Because apparently no two academic authors can agree on how to write a paper.</p><p>Here are some excellent topics:</p><h3>1. Linear Regression</h3><p><strong>Contains:</strong></p><ul><li><p>Matrix equations</p></li><li><p>Statistics</p></li><li><p>Greek symbols</p></li><li><p>Tables</p></li><li><p>Numbered equations</p></li></ul><p><strong>Equations:</strong><br>[<br>y = \beta_0 + \beta_1x + \epsilon<br>]</p><p>[<br>\hat{\beta}=(X^TX)^{-1}X^Ty<br>]</p><hr><h3>2. Logistic Regression</h3><p><strong>Contains:</strong></p><ul><li><p>Probability equations</p></li><li><p>Sigmoid function</p></li><li><p>Loss function</p></li><li><p>Binary classification</p></li></ul><p><strong>Equations</strong></p><p>[<br>P(y=1|x)=\frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}<br>]</p><p>[<br>J(\theta)=<br>-\frac1m<br>\sum_{i=1}^{m}<br>\left[<br>y_i\log(h_\theta(x_i))<br>+<br>(1-y_i)\log(1-h_\theta(x_i))<br>\right]<br>]</p><hr><h3>3. Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN)</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Images</p></li><li><p>Kernels</p></li><li><p>Feature maps</p></li><li><p>Convolution equations</p></li></ul><p>Equation</p><h1>[<br>S(i,j)</h1><h1>(X*K)(i,j)</h1><p>\sum_m\sum_n<br>X(i+m,j+n)K(m,n)<br>]</p><hr><h3>4. Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN)</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Time-series equations</p></li><li><p>Recursive formulas</p></li><li><p>Hidden states</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>h_t</h1><p>\tanh(W_hh_{t-1}+W_xx_t+b)<br>]</p><hr><h3>5. Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Four gate equations</p></li><li><p>Multiple matrices</p></li><li><p>Sigmoid</p></li><li><p>tanh</p></li></ul><p>Example</p><p>[<br>f_t=\sigma(W_fx_t+U_fh_{t-1}+b_f)<br>]</p><p>[<br>c_t=f_t\odot c_{t-1}+i_t\odot\tilde c_t<br>]</p><hr><h3>6. Gradient Descent</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Optimization equations</p></li><li><p>Algorithm steps</p></li><li><p>Mathematical notation</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>\theta</h1><h2>\theta</h2><p>\alpha<br>\nabla J(\theta)<br>]</p><hr><h3>7. Support Vector Machines (SVM)</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Optimization</p></li><li><p>Constraints</p></li><li><p>Kernel functions</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>f(x)</h1><p>w^Tx+b<br>]</p><hr><h3>8. Principal Component Analysis (PCA)</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Eigenvalues</p></li><li><p>Covariance matrices</p></li><li><p>Matrix algebra</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>C</h1><p>\frac1nX^TX<br>]</p><p>[<br>Cv=\lambda v<br>]</p><hr><h3>9. TRANSFORMED:

<!--StartFragment--><p>If you're testing an academic formatter, parser, or paste engine, you want reports that stress different formatting patterns. A report on Transformers is only one case. The real challenge comes from documents containing equations, lists, tables, code, references, figures, and mixed formatting. Because apparently no two academic authors can agree on how to write a paper.</p><p>Here are some excellent topics:</p><h3>1. Linear Regression</h3><p><strong>Contains:</strong></p><ul><li><p>Matrix equations</p></li><li><p>Statistics</p></li><li><p>Greek symbols</p></li><li><p>Tables</p></li><li><p>Numbered equations</p></li></ul><p><strong>Equations:</strong><br>[<br>y = \beta_0 + \beta_1x + \epsilon<br>]</p><p>[<br>\hat{\beta}=(X^TX)^{-1}X^Ty<br>]</p><h3>2. Logistic Regression</h3><p><strong>Contains:</strong></p><ul><li><p>Probability equations</p></li><li><p>Sigmoid function</p></li><li><p>Loss function</p></li><li><p>Binary classification</p></li></ul><p><strong>Equations</strong></p><p>[<br>P(y=1|x)=\frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}<br>]</p><p>[<br>J(\theta)=<br>-\frac1m<br>\sum_{i=1}^{m}<br>\left[<br>y_i\log(h_\theta(x_i))<br>+<br>(1-y_i)\log(1-h_\theta(x_i))<br>\right]<br>]</p><h3>3. ORIGINAL:
<html>
<body>
<!--StartFragment--><html><head></head><body><p>If you're looking for reports rich in <strong>simple engineering, computer science, mathematics, and AI formulas</strong> (rather than only advanced matrix equations), here is a large collection organized by subject. These are excellent for testing equation parsing and document formatting.</p><hr><h1>Software Engineering</h1><h3>Turnaround Time</h3><p>[<br>\text{Turnaround Time} = \text{Completion Time} - \text{Arrival Time}<br>]</p><h3>Waiting Time</h3><p>[<br>\text{Waiting Time} = \text{Turnaround Time} - \text{Burst Time}<br>]</p><h3>Response Time</h3><p>[<br>\text{Response Time} = \text{First Response} - \text{Arrival Time}<br>]</p><h3>CPU Utilization</h3><h1>[<br>\text{CPU Utilization}</h1><p>\frac{\text{CPU Busy Time}}{\text{Total Time}}<br>\times100%<br>]</p><h3>Throughput</h3><h1>[<br>\text{Throughput}</h1><p>\frac{\text{Number of Processes}}{\text{Total Time}}<br>]</p><hr><h1>Operating Systems</h1><h3>Effective Access Time</h3><h1>[<br>EAT</h1><p>(1-p)\times m<br>+<br>p\times P<br>]</p><p>where</p><ul><li><p>(m) = memory access time</p></li><li><p>(P) = page fault service time</p></li><li><p>(p) = page fault probability</p></li></ul><hr><h3>Speedup</h3><h1>[<br>S</h1><p>\frac{\text{Old Execution Time}}<br>{\text{New Execution Time}}<br>]</p><hr><h1>Computer Networks</h1><h3>Transmission Delay</h3><h1>[<br>T_t</h1><p>\frac{L}{R}<br>]</p><p>where</p><ul><li><p>(L) = packet length</p></li><li><p>(R) = transmission rate</p></li></ul><hr><h3>Propagation Delay</h3><h1>[<br>T_p</h1><p>\frac{d}{v}<br>]</p><hr><h3>Total Delay</h3><h1>[<br>D</h1><p>T_t<br>+<br>T_p<br>+<br>T_q<br>+<br>T_{proc}<br>]</p><hr><h3>Bandwidth Utilization</h3><h1>[<br>U</h1><p>\frac{\text{Useful Data}}<br>{\text{Total Bandwidth}}<br>]</p><hr><h1>Database Systems</h1><h3>Disk Access Time</h3><h1>[<br>T</h1><p>T_s<br>+<br>T_r<br>+<br>T_t<br>]</p><p>where</p><ul><li><p>Seek Time</p></li><li><p>Rotational Latency</p></li><li><p>Transfer Time</p></li></ul><hr><h1>Software Testing</h1><h3>Defect Density</h3><h1>[<br>\text{Defect Density}</h1><p>\frac{\text{Number of Defects}}<br>{\text{KLOC}}<br>]</p><hr><h3>Test Coverage</h3><h1>[<br>\text{Coverage}</h1><p>\frac{\text{Executed Statements}}<br>{\text{Total Statements}}<br>\times100%<br>]</p><hr><h3>Defect Removal Efficiency</h3><h1>[<br>DRE</h1><p>\frac{\text{Defects Removed}}<br>{\text{Total Defects}}<br>\times100%<br>]</p><hr><h1>Compiler Design</h1><h3>Compression Ratio</h3><h1>[<br>CR</h1><p>\frac{\text{Original Size}}<br>{\text{Compressed Size}}<br>]</p><hr><h1>Artificial Intelligence</h1><h3>Sigmoid Function</h3><h1>[<br>\sigma(x)</h1><p>\frac1{1+e^{-x}}<br>]</p><hr><h3>ReLU</h3><h1>[<br>f(x)</h1><p>\max(0,x)<br>]</p><hr><h3>Softmax</h3><h1>[<br>P_i</h1><p>\frac{e^{x_i}}<br>{\sum_j e^{x_j}}<br>]</p><hr><h3>Mean Squared Error</h3><h1>[<br>MSE</h1><p>\frac1n<br>\sum_{i=1}^{n}<br>(y_i-\hat y_i)^2<br>]</p><hr><h3>Cross Entropy Loss</h3><h1>[<br>L</h1><p>-\sum_i<br>y_i<br>\log(\hat y_i)<br>]</p><hr><h3>Gradient Descent</h3><h1>[<br>\theta</h1><h2>\theta</h2><p>\alpha<br>\nabla J(\theta)<br>]</p><hr><h1>Statistics</h1><h3>Mean</h3><h1>[<br>\mu</h1><p>\frac1n<br>\sum_{i=1}^{n}<br>x_i<br>]</p><hr><h3>Variance</h3><h1>[<br>\sigma^2</h1><p>\frac1n<br>\sum_{i=1}^{n}<br>(x_i-\mu)^2<br>]</p><hr><h3>Standard Deviation</h3><h1>[<br>\sigma</h1><p>\sqrt{\sigma^2}<br>]</p><hr><h3>Probability</h3><h1>[<br>P(A)</h1><p>\frac{\text{Favorable Outcomes}}<br>{\text{Total Outcomes}}<br>]</p><hr><h1>Digital Electronics</h1><h3>Binary to Decimal</h3><h1>[<br>N</h1><p>\sum_i<br>b_i2^i<br>]</p><hr><h3>Boolean Identity</h3><p>[<br>A+A=A<br>]</p><p>[<br>A\cdot1=A<br>]</p><p>[<br>A+0=A<br>]</p><hr><h1>Electrical Engineering</h1><h3>Ohm's Law</h3><h1>[<br>V</h1><p>IR<br>]</p><hr><h3>Electric Power</h3><h1>[<br>P</h1><p>VI<br>]</p><hr><h3>AC Power</h3><h1>[<br>P</h1><p>VI\cos\phi<br>]</p><hr><h3>Electrical Energy</h3><h1>[<br>E</h1><p>Pt<br>]</p><hr><h3>Efficiency</h3><h1>[<br>\eta</h1><p>\frac{\text{Output}}<br>{\text{Input}}<br>\times100%<br>]</p><hr><h1>AC Machines</h1><h3>Synchronous Speed</h3><h1>[<br>N_s</h1><p>\frac{120f}{P}<br>]</p><hr><h3>Slip</h3><h1>[<br>s</h1><p>\frac{N_s-N_r}{N_s}<br>]</p><hr><h3>Rotor Frequency</h3><h1>[<br>f_r</h1><p>sf<br>]</p><hr><h1>Physics</h1><h3>Newton's Second Law</h3><h1>[<br>F</h1><p>ma<br>]</p><hr><h3>Kinetic Energy</h3><h1>[<br>KE</h1><p>\frac12mv^2<br>]</p><hr><h3>Potential Energy</h3><h1>[<br>PE</h1><p>mgh<br>]</p><hr><h3>Momentum</h3><h1>[<br>p</h1><p>mv<br>]</p><hr><h3>Density</h3><h1>[<br>\rho</h1><p>\frac{m}{V}<br>]</p><hr><h1>Mathematics</h1><h3>Distance Formula</h3><h1>[<br>d</h1><p>\sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2+(y_2-y_1)^2}<br>]</p><hr><h3>Quadratic Formula</h3><h1>[<br>x</h1><p>\frac{-b\pm\sqrt{b^2-4ac}}<br>{2a}<br>]</p><hr><h3>Area of Circle</h3><h1>[<br>A</h1><p>\pi r^2<br>]</p><hr><h3>Circumference</h3><h1>[<br>C</h1><p>2\pi r<br>]</p><hr><h3>Simple Interest</h3><h1>[<br>SI</h1><p>\frac{PRT}{100}<br>]</p><hr><h3>Compound Interest</h3><h1>[<br>A</h1><p>P<br>\left(<br>1+\frac{r}{n}<br>\right)^{nt}<br>]</p><hr><h3>Percentage</h3><h1>[<br>\text{Percentage}</h1><p>\frac{\text{Obtained Marks}}<br>{\text{Total Marks}}<br>\times100<br>]</p><hr><h3>Average</h3><h1>[<br>\text{Average}</h1><p>\frac{\text{Sum of Values}}<br>{\text{Number of Values}}<br>]</p><hr><p>These kinds of formulas are excellent for testing academic document parsing because they include fractions, superscripts, subscripts, Greek symbols, summations, percentages, matrices, and inline variables without being overwhelmingly complex. A good formatter should preserve all of these correctly whether they originate from Markdown, LaTeX, PDFs, Word documents, or copied web pages.</p></body></html><!--EndFragment-->

<!--StartFragment--><p>If you're looking for reports rich in <strong>simple engineering, computer science, mathematics, and AI formulas</strong> (rather than only advanced matrix equations), here is a large collection organized by subject. <!--StartFragment--><html><head></head><body><p>If you're testing an academic formatter, parser, or paste engine, you want reports that stress different formatting patterns. A report on Transformers is only one case. The real challenge comes from documents containing equations, lists, tables, code, references, figures, and mixed formatting. Because apparently no two academic authors can agree on how to write a paper.</p><p>Here are some excellent topics:</p><h3>1. Linear Regression</h3><p><strong>Contains:</strong></p><ul><li><p>Matrix equations</p></li><li><p>Statistics</p></li><li><p>Greek symbols</p></li><li><p>Tables</p></li><li><p>Numbered equations</p></li></ul><p><strong>Equations:</strong><br>[<br>y = \beta_0 + \beta_1x + \epsilon<br>]</p><p>[<br>\hat{\beta}=(X^TX)^{-1}X^Ty<br>]</p><hr><h3>2. Logistic Regression</h3><p><strong>Contains:</strong></p><ul><li><p>Probability equations</p></li><li><p>Sigmoid function</p></li><li><p>Loss function</p></li><li><p>Binary classification</p></li></ul><p><strong>Equations</strong></p><p>[<br>P(y=1|x)=\frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}<br>]</p><p>[<br>J(\theta)=<br>-\frac1m<br>\sum_{i=1}^{m}<br>\left[<br>y_i\log(h_\theta(x_i))<br>+<br>(1-y_i)\log(1-h_\theta(x_i))<br>\right]<br>]</p><hr><h3>3. Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN)</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Images</p></li><li><p>Kernels</p></li><li><p>Feature maps</p></li><li><p>Convolution equations</p></li></ul><p>Equation</p><h1>[<br>S(i,j)</h1><h1>(X*K)(i,j)</h1><p>\sum_m\sum_n<br>X(i+m,j+n)K(m,n)<br>]</p><hr><h3>4. Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN)</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Time-series equations</p></li><li><p>Recursive formulas</p></li><li><p>Hidden states</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>h_t</h1><p>\tanh(W_hh_{t-1}+W_xx_t+b)<br>]</p><hr><h3>5. Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Four gate equations</p></li><li><p>Multiple matrices</p></li><li><p>Sigmoid</p></li><li><p>tanh</p></li></ul><p>Example</p><p>[<br>f_t=\sigma(W_fx_t+U_fh_{t-1}+b_f)<br>]</p><p>[<br>c_t=f_t\odot c_{t-1}+i_t\odot\tilde c_t<br>]</p><hr><h3>6. Gradient Descent</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Optimization equations</p></li><li><p>Algorithm steps</p></li><li><p>Mathematical notation</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>\theta</h1><h2>\theta</h2><p>\alpha<br>\nabla J(\theta)<br>]</p><hr><h3>7. Support Vector Machines (SVM)</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Optimization</p></li><li><p>Constraints</p></li><li><p>Kernel functions</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>f(x)</h1><p>w^Tx+b<br>]</p><hr><h3>8. Principal Component Analysis (PCA)</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Eigenvalues</p></li><li><p>Covariance matrices</p></li><li><p>Matrix algebra</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>C</h1><p>\frac1nX^TX<br>]</p><p>[<br>Cv=\lambda v<br>]</p><hr><h3>9. K-Means Clustering</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Algorithm</p></li><li><p>Distance equations</p></li><li><p>Iterative procedure</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>J</h1><p>\sum_{i=1}^{k}<br>\sum_{x\in C_i}<br>|x-\mu_i|^2<br>]</p><hr><h3>10. Decision Trees</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Information Gain</p></li><li><p>Entropy</p></li><li><p>Algorithms</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>Entropy(S)</h1><ul><li></li></ul><p>\sum_ip_i\log_2p_i<br>]</p><hr><h3>11. Naive Bayes</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Bayes theorem</p></li><li><p>Conditional probability</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>P(A|B)</h1><p>\frac{P(B|A)P(A)}{P(B)}<br>]</p><hr><h3>12. Graph Neural Networks (GNN)</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Graph notation</p></li><li><p>Matrix equations</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>H^{(l+1)}</h1><p>\sigma<br>(<br>\hat D^{-1/2}<br>\hat A<br>\hat D^{-1/2}<br>H^{(l)}<br>W^{(l)}<br>)<br>]</p><hr><h3>13. Diffusion Models</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Probability</p></li><li><p>Noise schedules</p></li><li><p>Gaussian equations</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>q(x_t|x_{t-1})</h1><p>\mathcal N<br>(<br>\sqrt{1-\beta_t}x_{t-1},<br>\beta_tI<br>)<br>]</p><hr><h3>14. Generative Adversarial Networks (GAN)</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Min-max optimization</p></li><li><p>Probability</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>\min_G<br>\max_D<br>V(D,G)</h1><p>\mathbb E[\log D(x)]<br>+<br>\mathbb E[\log(1-D(G(z)))]<br>]</p><hr><h3>15. Reinforcement Learning</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Bellman equations</p></li><li><p>Policies</p></li><li><p>Rewards</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>Q(s,a)</h1><p>r<br>+<br>\gamma<br>\max_{a'}<br>Q(s',a')<br>]</p><hr><h3>16. Quantum Computing</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Dirac notation</p></li><li><p>Matrices</p></li><li><p>Complex numbers</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>|\psi\rangle</h1><p>\alpha|0\rangle<br>+<br>\beta|1\rangle<br>]</p><hr><h3>17. Fourier Transform</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Integrals</p></li><li><p>Complex exponentials</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>F(\omega)</h1><p>\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}<br>f(t)e^{-j\omega t}dt<br>]</p><hr><h3>18. AC Induction Motor</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Engineering equations</p></li><li><p>Tables</p></li><li><p>Diagrams</p></li><li><p>Symbols</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>N_s</h1><p>\frac{120f}{P}<br>]</p><h1>[<br>s</h1><p>\frac{N_s-N_r}{N_s}<br>]</p><hr><h3>19. Software Testing</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>UML diagrams</p></li><li><p>Tables</p></li><li><p>Bullet lists</p></li><li><p>Process flows</p></li><li><p>IEEE references</p></li></ul><p>Little mathematical content, making it a good contrast to AI-heavy papers.</p><hr><h3>20. Operating Systems</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Scheduling algorithms</p></li><li><p>Code blocks</p></li><li><p>Tables</p></li><li><p>Memory management formulas</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>Turnaround</h1><h2>Completion</h2><p>Arrival<br>]</p><hr><h2>For stress-testing your formatter</h2><p>If your goal is to build something like FormatMagic or a robust academic paste engine, I'd assemble a corpus rather than rely on a handful of reports. Include documents that exercise different structures:</p><table><thead><tr><th>Document Type</th><th>Tests</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td>Transformer Architecture</td><td>Inline math, display math, headings</td></tr><tr><td>Linear Regression</td><td>Matrix equations</td></tr><tr><td>CNN</td><td>Figures, equations</td></tr><tr><td>Software Testing</td><td>Tables, nested lists</td></tr><tr><td>AC Induction Motor</td><td>Engineering formulas, units</td></tr><tr><td>Research Paper (IEEE)</td><td>References, citations, two-column layout</td></tr><tr><td>Thesis Chapter</td><td>Long sections, footnotes</td></tr><tr><td>Markdown README</td><td>Fenced code, lists</td></tr><tr><td>Programming Tutorial</td><td>Syntax-highlighted code blocks</td></tr><tr><td>Physics Notes</td><td>Integrals, summations, Greek symbols</td></tr><tr><td>Chemistry Notes</td><td>Chemical formulas, subscripts</td></tr><tr><td>Economics Paper</td><td>Tables, percentages, charts</td></tr><tr><td>Statistics Notes</td><td>Probability notation and distributions</td></tr></tbody></table><p>A formatter that can faithfully handle all of those is much closer to a production-quality document pipeline than one that only succeeds on neatly formatted AI reports.</p></body></html><!--EndFragment-->

<!--StartFragment--><p>If you're testing an academic formatter, parser, or paste engine, you want reports that stress different formatting patterns. A report on Transformers is only one case. Because apparently no two academic authors can agree on how to write a paper.</p><p>Here are some excellent topics:</p><h3>1. Linear Regression</h3><p><strong>Contains:</strong></p><ul><li><p>Matrix equations</p></li><li><p>Statistics</p></li><li><p>Greek symbols</p></li><li><p>Tables</p></li><li><p>Numbered equations</p></li></ul><p><strong>Equations:</strong><br>[<br>y = \beta_0 + \beta_1x + \epsilon<br>]</p><p>[<br>\hat{\beta}=(X^TX)^{-1}X^Ty<br>]</p><h3>2. Logistic Regression</h3><p><strong>Contains:</strong></p><ul><li><p>Probability equations</p></li><li><p>Sigmoid function</p></li><li><p>Loss function</p></li><li><p>Binary classification</p></li></ul><p><strong>Equations</strong></p><p>[<br>P(y=1|x)=\frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}<br>]</p><p>[<br>J(\theta)=<br>-\frac1m<br>\sum_{i=1}^{m}<br>\left[<br>y_i\log(h_\theta(x_i))<br>+<br>(1-y_i)\log(1-h_\theta(x_i))<br>\right]<br>]</p><h3>3. Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN)</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Images</p></li><li><p>Kernels</p></li><li><p>Feature maps</p></li><li><p>Convolution equations</p></li></ul><p>Equation</p><h1>[<br>S(i,j)</h1><h1>(X*K)(i,j)</h1><p>\sum_m\sum_n<br>X(i+m,j+n)K(m,n)<br>]</p><h3>4. Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN)</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Time-series equations</p></li><li><p>Recursive formulas</p></li><li><p>Hidden states</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>h_t</h1><p>\tanh(W_hh_{t-1}+W_xx_t+b)<br>]</p><h3>5. Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Four gate equations</p></li><li><p>Multiple matrices</p></li><li><p>Sigmoid</p></li><li><p>tanh</p></li></ul><p>Example</p><p>[<br>f_t=\sigma(W_fx_t+U_fh_{t-1}+b_f)<br>]</p><p>[<br>c_t=f_t\odot c_{t-1}+i_t\odot\tilde c_t<br>]</p><h3>6. Gradient Descent</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Optimization equations</p></li><li><p>Algorithm steps</p></li><li><p>Mathematical notation</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>\theta</h1><h2>\theta</h2><p>\alpha<br>\nabla J(\theta)<br>]</p><h3>7. Support Vector Machines (SVM)</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Optimization</p></li><li><p>Constraints</p></li><li><p>Kernel functions</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>f(x)</h1><p>w^Tx+b<br>]</p><h3>8. Principal Component Analysis (PCA)</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Eigenvalues</p></li><li><p>Covariance matrices</p></li><li><p>Matrix algebra</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>C</h1><p>\frac1nX^TX<br>]</p><p>[<br>Cv=\lambda v<br>]</p><h3>9. K-Means Clustering</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Algorithm</p></li><li><p>Distance equations</p></li><li><p>Iterative procedure</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>J</h1><p>\sum_{i=1}^{k}<br>\sum_{x\in C_i}<br>|x-\mu_i|^2<br>]</p><h3>10. Decision Trees</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Information Gain</p></li><li><p>Entropy</p></li><li><p>Algorithms</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>Entropy(S)</h1><ul><li></li></ul><p>\sum_ip_i\log_2p_i<br>]</p><h3>11. Naive Bayes</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Bayes theorem</p></li><li><p>Conditional probability</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>P(A|B)</h1><p>\frac{P(B|A)P(A)}{P(B)}<br>]</p><h3>12. Graph Neural Networks (GNN)</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Graph notation</p></li><li><p>Matrix equations</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>H^{(l+1)}</h1><p>\sigma<br>(<br>\hat D^{-1/2}<br>\hat A<br>\hat D^{-1/2}<br>H^{(l)}<br>W^{(l)}<br>)<br>]</p><h3>13. Diffusion Models</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Probability</p></li><li><p>Noise schedules</p></li><li><p>Gaussian equations</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>q(x_t|x_{t-1})</h1><p>\mathcal N<br>(<br>\sqrt{1-\beta_t}x_{t-1},<br>\beta_tI<br>)<br>]</p><h3>14. Generative Adversarial Networks (GAN)</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Min-max optimization</p></li><li><p>Probability</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>\min_G<br>\max_D<br>V(D,G)</h1><p>\mathbb E[\log D(x)]<br>+<br>\mathbb E[\log(1-D(G(z)))]<br>]</p><h3>15. Reinforcement Learning</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Bellman equations</p></li><li><p>Policies</p></li><li><p>Rewards</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>Q(s,a)</h1><p>r<br>+<br>\gamma<br>\max_{a'}<br>Q(s',a')<br>]</p><h3>16. Quantum Computing</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Dirac notation</p></li><li><p>Matrices</p></li><li><p>Complex numbers</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>|\psi\rangle</h1><p>\alpha|0\rangle<br>+<br>\beta|1\rangle<br>]</p><h3>17. Fourier Transform</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Integrals</p></li><li><p>Complex exponentials</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>F(\omega)</h1><p>\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}<br>f(t)e^{-j\omega t}dt<br>]</p><h3>18. AC Induction Motor</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Engineering equations</p></li><li><p>Tables</p></li><li><p>Diagrams</p></li><li><p>Symbols</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>N_s</h1><p>\frac{120f}{P}<br>]</p><h1>[<br>s</h1><p>\frac{N_s-N_r}{N_s}<br>]</p><h3>19. Software Testing</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>UML diagrams</p></li><li><p>Tables</p></li><li><p>Bullet lists</p></li><li><p>Process flows</p></li><li><p>IEEE references</p></li></ul><p>Little mathematical content, making it a good contrast to AI-heavy papers.</p><h3>20. Operating Systems</h3><p>Contains</p><ul><li><p>Scheduling algorithms</p></li><li><p>Code blocks</p></li><li><p>Tables</p></li><li><p>Memory management formulas</p></li></ul><h1>[<br>Turnaround</h1><h2>Completion</h2><p>Arrival<br>]</p><h2>For stress-testing your formatter</h2><p>If your goal is to build something like FormatMagic or a robust academic paste engine, I'd assemble a corpus rather than rely on a handful of reports. Include documents that exercise different structures:</p><table><thead><tr><th>Document Type</th><th>Tests</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td>Transformer Architecture</td><td>Inline math, display math, headings</td></tr><tr><td>Linear Regression</td><td>Matrix equations</td></tr><tr><td>CNN</td><td>Figures, equations</td></tr><tr><td>Software Testing</td><td>Tables, nested lists</td></tr><tr><td>AC Induction Motor</td><td>Engineering formulas, units</td></tr><tr><td>Research Paper (IEEE)</td><td>References, citations, two-column layout</td></tr><tr><td>Thesis Chapter</td><td>Long sections, footnotes</td></tr><tr><td>Markdown README</td><td>Fenced code, lists</td></tr><tr><td>Programming Tutorial</td><td>Syntax-highlighted code blocks</td></tr><tr><td>Physics Notes</td><td>Integrals, summations, Greek symbols</td></tr><tr><td>Chemistry Notes</td><td>Chemical formulas, subscripts</td></tr><tr><td>Economics Paper</td><td>Tables, percentages, charts</td></tr><tr><td>Statistics Notes</td><td>Probability notation and distributions</td></tr></tbody></table><p>A formatter that can faithfully handle all of those is much closer to a production-quality document pipeline than one that only succeeds on neatly formatted AI reports.</p>

<!--StartFragment--><h3 data-section-id="vp1wy2" data-start="1537" data-end="1556" class="PDq2pG_selectionAnchorContainer">CPU Utilization<span aria-hidden="true" class="PDq2pG_selectionAnchor"></span></h3>

<span class="katex-display"><span class="katex"><span class="katex-mathml"><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="block"><semantics><mrow><mtext>CPU&nbsp;Utilization</mtext><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mtext>CPU&nbsp;Busy&nbsp;Time</mtext><mtext>Total&nbsp;Time</mtext></mfrac><mo>×</mo><mn>100</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">%</mi></mrow><annotation encoding="application/x-tex">\text{CPU Utilization}

{\text{Total Time}}
\times100\%</annotation></semantics></math></span><span class="katex-html" aria-hidden="true"><span class="base"><span class="strut"></span><span class="mord text"><span class="mord">CPU&nbsp;Utilization</span></span><span class="mspace"></span><span class="mrel">=</span><span class="mspace"></span></span><span class="base"><span class="strut"></span><span class="mord"><span class="mopen nulldelimiter"></span><span class="mfrac"><span class="vlist-t vlist-t2"><span class="vlist-r"><span class="vlist"><span><span class="pstrut"></span><span class="mord"><span class="mord text"><span class="mord">Total&nbsp;Time</span></span></span></span><span><span class="pstrut"></span><span class="frac-line"></span></span><span><span class="pstrut"></span><span class="mord"><span class="mord text"><span class="mord">CPU&nbsp;Busy&nbsp;Time</span></span></span></span></span><span class="vlist-s">​</span></span><span class="vlist-r"><span class="vlist"><span></span></span></span></span></span><span class="mclose nulldelimiter"></span></span><span class="mspace"></span><span class="mbin">×</span><span class="mspace"></span></span><span class="base"><span class="strut"></span><span class="mord">100%</span></span></span></span></span><!--EndFragment-->

<!--StartFragment--><h3 data-section-id="vp1wy2" data-start="1537" data-end="1556" class="PDq2pG_selectionAnchorContainer">CPU Utilization<span aria-hidden="true" class="PDq2pG_selectionAnchor"></span></h3>
<p data-start="1558" data-end="1628">CPU utilization indicates how efficiently the processor is being used.</p>
<span class="katex-display"><span class="katex"><span class="katex-mathml"><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="block"><semantics><mrow><mtext>CPU&nbsp;Utilization</mtext><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mtext>CPU&nbsp;Busy&nbsp;Time</mtext><mtext>Total&nbsp;Time</mtext></mfrac><mo>×</mo><mn>100</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">%</mi></mrow><annotation encoding="application/x-tex">\text{CPU Utilization}

{\text{Total Time}}
\times100\%</annotation></semantics></math></span></span></span><!--EndFragment-->

<p data-start="1738" data-end="1810">Throughput measures the number of processes completed in a unit of time.</p>
<span class="katex-display"><span class="katex"><span class="katex-mathml"><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="block"><semantics><mrow><mtext>Throughput</mtext><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mtext>Number&nbsp;of&nbsp;Processes</mtext><mtext>Total&nbsp;Time</mtext></mfrac></mrow><annotation encoding="application/x-tex">\text{Throughput}

{\text{Total Time}}</annotation></semantics></math></span><span class="katex-html" aria-hidden="true"><span class="base"><span class="strut"></span><span class="mord text"><span class="mord">Throughput</span></span><span class="mspace"></span><span class="mrel">=</span><span class="mspace"></span></span><span class="base"><span class="strut"></span><span class="mord"><span class="mopen nulldelimiter"></span><span class="mfrac"><span class="vlist-t vlist-t2"><span class="vlist-r"><span class="vlist"><span><span class="pstrut"></span><span class="mord"><span class="mord text"><span class="mord">Total&nbsp;Time</span></span></span></span><span><span class="pstrut"></span><span class="frac-line"></span></span><span><span class="pstrut"></span><span class="mord"><span class="mord text"><span class="mord">Number&nbsp;of&nbsp;Processes</span></span></span></span></span><span class="vlist-s">​</span></span><span class="vlist-r"><span class="vlist"></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><!--EndFragment-->
